title: Code Robots That Actually Move

modules.title: How AI and Robotics Actually Fit Together
modules.sub: Mapping the relationship between software intelligence and physical machines
modules.description: Learners build a mental model of how AI layers onto robotics — where machine learning ends and mechanical control begins. You survey real-world examples from warehouse bots to autonomous vehicles to see how the pieces connect. By the end, you can describe the AI-robotics stack in plain language to anyone.

modules.title: Sensors, Actuators, and the Loop That Makes Robots Think
modules.sub: Understanding the sense-decide-act cycle that drives every robot
modules.description: You explore how robots perceive the world through sensors and respond through actuators, tracing the feedback loop that ties them together. Learners examine common hardware — ultrasonic sensors, servos, IMUs — and learn what data each one produces. You leave knowing how to read a robot's inputs and outputs and reason about what can go wrong.

modules.title: Using Computer Vision to Give Robots Eyes
modules.sub: Connecting cameras and vision models to real robot perception
modules.description: Learners set up a basic computer vision pipeline using open tools like OpenCV and pre-trained models to detect objects or track movement. You experiment with how image data gets processed into decisions a robot can act on. By the end, you can wire a camera feed to a simple decision trigger and explain the latency and accuracy trade-offs involved.

modules.title: Training Simple Models for Robot Decision-Making
modules.sub: Building lightweight AI models that tell robots what to do next
modules.description: You train a small classification or reinforcement learning model on sensor or image data and evaluate how well it drives robot behavior. Learners work through the full loop: collect data, train, test, and observe the robot's response. You develop intuition for when a simple model is good enough versus when you need something more powerful.

modules.title: ROS and the Tools the Industry Actually Uses
modules.sub: Getting hands-on with the software stack professionals rely on
modules.description: Learners get oriented in ROS (Robot Operating System), understanding why it exists and how its nodes, topics, and messages structure real robotics projects. You run basic ROS commands, inspect a live topic, and see how your earlier sensor and vision work maps onto professional workflows. You walk away able to navigate a ROS environment and talk credibly about industry tooling.

modules.title: Building and Testing a Basic Autonomous Behavior
modules.sub: Integrating your skills into one robot that acts on its own
modules.description: Learners design and implement a single autonomous behavior — such as obstacle avoidance, line following, or object-directed movement — combining sensors, a trained model, and actuation. You test the behavior iteratively, debug failures, and document what works and what breaks. This module is where everything clicks: you see your code make something physical move with purpose.

modules.title: AI Robotics Careers: Where the Jobs Are Right Now
modules.sub: Decoding the job landscape and what employers actually want
modules.description: You survey the real career paths in AI robotics — from robotics software engineer to perception specialist to automation consultant — and learn what skills each role prioritizes. Learners analyze real job postings and identify which parts of this course map directly to employer requirements. You finish with a clear picture of where you fit now and what to build toward next.

modules.title: Your Capstone: Design an AI-Driven Robot Task
modules.sub: Proposing and presenting an original AI robotics project from scratch
modules.description: Learners design an original AI-driven robot task — specifying the hardware, sensor inputs, AI approach, and expected behavior — and present it as a structured project proposal. You justify your design decisions, anticipate failure points, and connect your concept to real industry tools and practices. This capstone demonstrates that you can think like a robotics engineer, not just follow a tutorial.

synopsis: Robotics used to mean expensive labs and PhD programs — AI has changed that, and you can start building intelligent physical systems today with open tools and cheap hardware. This course connects the software you already know to robots that move, sense, and decide.
rationale: AI-powered robotics is one of the fastest-growing career sectors for young engineers and makers, yet accessible entry points are almost absent from general AI literacy curricula.
learning_outcome: After this course, you can design and prototype a basic AI-driven robotic behavior using computer vision or sensor input, and speak fluently about the industry tools and job landscape.
